laasya_aduri.name: Laasya Aduri
laasya_aduri.summary:
Representing Illinois, Laasya Aduri has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 29 entries across 11 tournaments, Laasya is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Notably, Laasya has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Laasya especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Laasya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Veronica Ornatsky (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Laasya has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Laasya isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Laasya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
laasya_aduri.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Laasya Aduri, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Laasya has 29 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Laasya saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Laasya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Laasya has reached the finals 2 times — at 2024 Midwest CRC, 2022 Egret Midwest CRC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Laasya has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Laasya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Laasya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Laasya's texts. The go-to partner is Arthur Yan (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Arthur Yan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Laasya has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Laasya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Laasya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Laasya has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Laasya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Laasya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Laasya entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Laasya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 29 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Laasya. Laasya just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Laasya has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Laasya. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Laasya: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

laasya_polineni.name: Laasya Polineni
laasya_polineni.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Laasya Polineni is a U.S. Junior National Champion — crowned at the biggest event on the domestic calendar in XD U11. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 10 career victories across 21 tournaments, Laasya has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Notably, Laasya has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Laasya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (XD U11) • 3 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Laasya boasts a career +694 point differential, 46 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a biggest win margin of 35 points.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Laasya adapts to anyone:
  • Virat Satish (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (12 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Kavya Balaji (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Aadya Yanamandra (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in GD)
  • Felix Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)

Laasya has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Laasya has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Laasya has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 4 seasons and 10 titles, Laasya has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
laasya_polineni.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Laasya Polineni, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won XD U11. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 58 career entries across 21 tournaments, 10 total wins, and 48 losses. A 17% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Laasya has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2023 MassBad NE OLC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Laasya saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE NJBC CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Laasya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 7 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Laasya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 3 wins in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Laasya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Laasya has reached 13 championship matches across this career. That is 13 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 13 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 NE MassBad OLC, where Laasya entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U11, XD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (9 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Laasya does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Laasya has never won anything. But 13 finals losses versus 10 wins means Laasya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Laasya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Laasya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Laasya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Laasya's texts. The most successful partnership is with Virat Satish — 12 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Virat Satish deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Virat Satish a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Felix Liu has emotionally recovered.

Of Laasya's 10 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 4 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Laasya feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Then there is the seeding situation. Laasya has been seeded in 11 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Laasya takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 NE MassBad OLC in GS U13, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Laasya. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Laasya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Laasya is not just losing locally — Laasya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Polineni family travels cross-country so Laasya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Laasya has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Laasya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11, which means Laasya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Laasya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Laasya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 58 entries, and 48 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Laasya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Laasya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Laasya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Laasya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

laila_qiao_wang.name: Laila Qiao Wang
laila_qiao_wang.summary:
Laila Qiao Wang is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Laila competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
laila_qiao_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Laila Qiao Wang, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Laila is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 NorCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Laila has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Laila saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Laila plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Laila is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Laila wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Laila, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

lance_fuchia.name: Lance Fuchia
lance_fuchia.summary:
A California competitor, Lance Fuchia is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

What makes Lance especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • 1 ORC title.

In doubles, Lance has formed effective partnerships:
  • Kenny Yaowen Luu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Akshaya Ambily Ajan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)

The 2021-2022 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Lance is still ascending.

Lance has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Lance isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Lance's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
lance_fuchia.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lance Fuchia, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-5. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Lance has 12 career entries across 5 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Lance saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lance's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lance plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Lance is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner.

At Junior Nationals, Lance has shown up 2 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Lance has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lance has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lance showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17, which means Lance traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 12 entries, and 11 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lance looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lance's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lance has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lance. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

landon_leung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Landon Leung, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Landon is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Landon has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Landon saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 1 entries and a best of 65th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Landon has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Landon has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Landon showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Landon traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Landon entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Landon paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Landon keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Landon has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Landon, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Landon. We will be here, taking notes.

A true triple-threat, Landon competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Landon adapts to anyone:
  • Charlie W Gao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Landon has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Landon has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Landon brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
landon_ling.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Landon Ling, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Landon has 37 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Landon saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Landon plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 37 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Landon has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Landon's texts. The go-to partner is Haochen Zhang Li (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Ching Chun Fan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Landon has shown up 6 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Landon is not just losing locally — Landon is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ling family travels cross-country so Landon can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Landon has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Landon showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Landon traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Landon entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Landon paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 37 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Landon. Landon just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Landon has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Landon. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Landon: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Lanting has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Lanting. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. layton_wen.name: Layton Wen
layton_wen.summary:
Representing California, Layton Wen keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 7 times, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 14 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

What makes Layton especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Layton adapts to anyone:
  • Max Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Chang He — a trusted XD partner (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Layton has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Layton has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience, Layton brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
layton_wen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Layton Wen, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Layton has 33 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Layton saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 6 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Layton plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 33 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Layton has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Layton's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Chang He: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Chang He has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Muyang Li has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Layton has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Layton is not just losing locally — Layton is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wen family travels cross-country so Layton can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Layton has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Layton showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Layton traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NW ORC, where Layton entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Layton paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 6 seasons, 33 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Layton. Layton just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Layton has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Layton. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Layton: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

  • Aaron Liao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (6 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

  • Radhika Birur (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Allison Hsu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

leheng_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Leheng Xu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Leheng has 78 career entries across 28 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 1% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Leheng saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 NW OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Leheng's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 20 entries — a stretch where Leheng actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Leheng is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Leheng plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Leheng is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Leheng has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Leheng's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Karena Ling: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Karena Ling has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Karena Ling has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Leheng's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Leheng has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Leheng is not just losing locally — Leheng is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xu family travels cross-country so Leheng can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Leheng has 46 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Leheng showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Leheng traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Leheng entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Leheng paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 78 entries, and 77 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Leheng looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Leheng's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Leheng has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Leheng. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

lekhya_vajja.summary: Lekhya Vajja is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Lekhya's.
lekhya_vajja.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lekhya Vajja, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Lekhya is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 Play N Thrive MW CRC, and so far, the only thing Lekhya has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Lekhya saga began at 2026 Play N Thrive MW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Lekhya is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Lekhya's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Lekhya is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Lekhya is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Lekhya wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Lekhya, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

leland_shuo_gao.name: Leland Shuo Gao
leland_shuo_gao.summary:
Out of California, Leland Shuo Gao is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U13 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Leland competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

In doubles, Leland has formed effective partnerships:
  • Alysia Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
leland_shuo_gao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Leland Shuo Gao, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Leland is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Leland has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Leland saga began at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Leland plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Leland has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Leland showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Leland traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Leland entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Leland paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Leland is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Leland wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Leland, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

lena_chen.name: Lena Chen
lena_chen.summary:
Out of Texas, Lena Chen is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U11 level, with 10 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Lena especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Lena has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Darlene Axella Purwanto (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events)

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Lena's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
lena_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lena Chen, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Lena's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Lena keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Lena saga began at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lena plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Lena has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in XD U11 — we hope Jesse Lai Liu has emotionally recovered.

Lena has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lena has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U11, which means Lena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Lena entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Lena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Lena keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Lena has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Lena, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Lena. We will be here, taking notes.

lenny_zhang.name: Lenny Zhang
lenny_zhang.summary:
A California competitor, Lenny Zhang broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 18 entries across 9 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Lenny is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Lenny has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Zackary Tsui (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Nikhil Kandalam — 3 events in BD, best: #2, across 2 seasons

Lenny has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Lenny has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Lenny brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
lenny_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lenny Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Lenny has 18 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Lenny saga began at 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BD U19 - Position 5-8, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lenny's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 11 entries — a stretch where Lenny actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Lenny is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lenny plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Lenny is 0-for-9. Zero titles in 9 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Lenny and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Lenny needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Lenny has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Nikhil Kandalam (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U19 — we hope Zackary Tsui has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Lenny's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Lenny has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lenny is not just losing locally — Lenny is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Lenny can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lenny has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lenny showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Lenny traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Lenny entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Lenny paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 18 entries, and 17 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lenny looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lenny's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lenny has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lenny. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

2024-2025.t: 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC

leo_ye.name: Leo Ye
leo_ye.summary:
A California competitor, Leo Ye is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (BD U11, XD U11) puts Leo in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Leo has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Leo especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U11, XD U11) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Leo has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 18 different players across the career:
  • Jason Zhu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)
  • Reyansh Chalamalasetti (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

Leo has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Leo has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Leo consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Leo brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
leo_ye.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Leo Ye, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 54 career entries in 18 tournaments, Leo has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 53 entries where Leo drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 54 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Leo saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Leo experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Leo has reached the finals 2 times — at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Leo has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Leo plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 54 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Leo has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Leo's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Grace Cheng: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Grace Cheng has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Ayushi Raheja has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Leo entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Leo's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Leo and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Leo has competed 9 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Leo has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Leo is not just losing locally — Leo is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ye family travels cross-country so Leo can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Leo has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Leo showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Leo traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Leo entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Leo paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 54 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Leo. Leo just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Leo has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Leo. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Leo: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

leon_the_laugher_sewell.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Leon "The Laugher" Sewell, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Leon is right on schedule. Leon is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Leon's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Leon is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Leon has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Leon showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Capital NE OLC in BS U13, which means Leon traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Leon is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Leon wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Leon, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

leon_luo.name: Leon Luo
leon_luo.summary:
Representing New Jersey, Leon Luo has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U13 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 7 career titles across 31 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Notably, Leon has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Leon especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U13, BD U11, XD U11) • 3 ORC titles.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Leon boasts a biggest win margin of 32 points, a positive +398 point differential across the career, a solid 66% win rate over 74 matches.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Leon adapts to anyone:
  • Ethan Qiu (current primary partner) — 13 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (28 total events in BD/U13, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Mizuki Ikeuchi (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Leon is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Leon has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Leon has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 17 seeded entries across the career, Leon consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Leon is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
leon_luo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Leon Luo, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Leon is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Leon has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Leon would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Leon, we kept reading — all 88 entries across 31 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 8% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Leon held up a trophy, there were roughly 11 other times Leon walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Leon saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2023 South ORC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Leon's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 26 entries — a stretch where Leon actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Leon experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Leon has reached 10 championship matches across this career. That is 10 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 10 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (8 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Leon does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Leon has never won anything. But 10 finals losses versus 7 wins means Leon is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Leon plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Leon is 0-for-30. Zero titles in 30 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Leon and suddenly the wins start appearing — 6 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Leon needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Leon has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Leon's texts. The most successful partnership is with Ethan Qiu — 28 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Ethan Qiu deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Ethan Qiu a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Ethan Qiu has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 9 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Leon has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell.

Leon has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Leon is not just losing locally — Leon is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Luo family travels cross-country so Leon can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Leon has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Leon showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Leon traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Leon entered 3 events with results ranging from 3rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Leon paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 88 entries, and 81 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Leon looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Leon's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Leon has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Leon. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
