leslie_wang.name: Leslie Wang
leslie_wang.summary:
Leslie Wang has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BD U11. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Leslie belongs with the best in the country.

What makes Leslie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (BD U11) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Diving into the match data reveals: Leslie boasts 17 three-game battles — proving Leslie is no stranger to the pressure of a decider, a biggest win margin of 34 points, a career +522 point differential.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Leslie adapts to anyone:
  • Jeremy Wang (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (14 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Zelenia Shen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Reyansh Chalamalasetti (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Yuki Guan (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 3 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Leslie is still ascending.

Leslie has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Leslie has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Leslie has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Leslie is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
leslie_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Leslie Wang, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 69 career entries in 24 tournaments, Leslie has 3 titles — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 22 entries where Leslie drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Leslie saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Leslie's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 18 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Leslie experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Leslie has reached 11 championship matches across this career. That is 11 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 11 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC, where Leslie entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13, BD U13). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (9 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Leslie does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Leslie has never won anything. But 11 finals losses versus 3 wins means Leslie is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Leslie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Leslie can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Leslie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Leslie's texts. The go-to partner is Jeremy Wang (14 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Joanna Deng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Leslie's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Leslie entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Leslie's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Leslie and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Leslie 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Leslie has been seeded in 17 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Leslie takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U13, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Leslie. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Leslie is not just losing locally — Leslie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Leslie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Leslie has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Leslie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13, which means Leslie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Leslie entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 29th, which is a polite way of saying Leslie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 69 entries, and 66 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Leslie looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Leslie's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Leslie has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Leslie. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

lexuan_wen.name: Lexuan Wen
lexuan_wen.summary:
Out of California, Lexuan Wen wears the gold from Junior Nationals — the single most important tournament for any American junior player in GD U15. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 19 career victories across 25 tournaments, Lexuan has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Lexuan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Lexuan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 5 singles titles, 11 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (GD U15) • 16 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Lexuan boasts a clutch 65% win rate in three-game matches (11/17), 51 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, an extraordinary 81% match win rate across 80 matches.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Lexuan adapts to anyone:
  • Shraddha Prakash (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in GD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jacob Ma (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Alice Meng (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 5 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Lexuan is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Lexuan has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Lexuan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Lexuan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 13 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 19 titles, Lexuan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
lexuan_wen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lexuan Wen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won GD U15. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 75 career entries across 25 tournaments, 19 total wins, and 56 losses. A 25% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Lexuan has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2021 NorCal CRC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Lexuan saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Lexuan burst onto the scene with a title in XD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Lexuan has delivered on some of those promises: 19 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Lexuan promised and what Lexuan has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 8 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Lexuan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 5 wins in 12 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Lexuan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Lexuan has reached 17 championship matches across this career. That is 17 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 17 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Lexuan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (8 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (9 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Lexuan does own 19 actual titles, so it is not like Lexuan has never won anything. But 17 finals losses versus 19 wins means Lexuan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lexuan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 11 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Lexuan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Lexuan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lexuan's texts. The most successful partnership is with Mihika Jaiswal — 10 events, 5 titles — and honestly, Mihika Jaiswal deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Mihika Jaiswal a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15 — we hope Clay Yang has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Lexuan has been seeded in 22 events and underperformed the seeding 7 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Lexuan takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Lexuan. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Lexuan of is playing it safe. Lexuan has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Lexuan has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 11 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Lexuan has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lexuan is not just losing locally — Lexuan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 25% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wen family travels cross-country so Lexuan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lexuan has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lexuan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in GS U13, which means Lexuan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 19 titles to the name, 75 entries, and 56 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lexuan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lexuan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lexuan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lexuan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

leyton_siew.name: Leyton Siew
leyton_siew.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Leyton Siew has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Leyton's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Leyton competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-4 finisher.

Leyton has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Jennifer Cui (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Leyton has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Leyton has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Leyton's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
leyton_siew.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Leyton Siew, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 19 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 19-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Leyton's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Leyton keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Leyton saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Leyton plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Leyton has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Leyton's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U19 — we hope Srikrishna Sriganesh has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Leyton is not just losing locally — Leyton is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Siew family travels cross-country so Leyton can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Leyton has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Leyton showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17, which means Leyton traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Leyton entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Leyton paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Leyton keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Leyton has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Leyton, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Leyton. We will be here, taking notes.

liam_xiao.name: Liam Xiao
liam_xiao.summary:
Representing New Jersey, Liam Xiao has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Liam's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Liam competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Liam boasts 38 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a clutch 62% win rate in three-game matches (10/16), a positive +348 point differential across the career.

With 23 different doubles partners over the years, Liam adapts to anyone:
  • Medha Sivani Gudavalli (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (9 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Jida Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Alicia Chang (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in XD)
  • Lukas Gabriel Bayani (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Liam is still ascending.

Liam has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Liam has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Liam consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, Liam is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
liam_xiao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Liam Xiao, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 88 career entries in 31 tournaments, Liam has 4 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 21 entries where Liam drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 88 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Liam saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Liam's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Liam experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Liam has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 MassBad NE OLC, where Liam entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, XD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Liam does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Liam has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 4 wins means Liam is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Liam plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Liam can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Liam has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Liam's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Medha Sivani Gudavalli: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Medha Sivani Gudavalli has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Zmy Gao has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Liam's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Liam entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Liam's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Liam and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Liam has shown up 8 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Liam has been seeded in 7 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Liam takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 South OLC in BS U11, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Liam. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Liam is not just losing locally — Liam is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xiao family travels cross-country so Liam can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Liam has 42 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Liam showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Liam traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Liam entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Liam paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 88 entries, and 84 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Liam looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Liam's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Liam has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Liam. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Lili has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Lili. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Lili: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

lilian_feng.name: Lilian Feng
lilian_feng.summary:
Out of New Jersey, Lilian Feng has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U13 GD, U13 GS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 16 career titles across 26 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Notably, Lilian has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Lilian is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 5 singles titles, 7 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 GD, U13 GS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (GD U11) • 8 ORC titles.

Lilian has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Keira Winarto (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (16 total events in GD/U13, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Edwin Huang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Having moved up to U15, Lilian is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Lilian brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Lilian has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Lilian has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Lilian has been seeded #1 or #2 in 11 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 16 titles, Lilian is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
lilian_feng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lilian Feng, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Lilian is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Lilian has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Lilian would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Lilian, we kept reading — all 74 entries across 26 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 22% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Lilian held up a trophy, there were roughly 3 other times Lilian walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Lilian saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 NE CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lilian's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 7 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Lilian actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Lilian is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Lilian experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Lilian has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Lilian does own 16 actual titles, so it is not like Lilian has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 16 wins means Lilian is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lilian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 7 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Lilian can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Lilian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lilian's texts. The most successful partnership is with Keira Winarto — 16 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Keira Winarto deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Keira Winarto a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13 — we hope Ethan Qiu has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Lilian has been seeded in 21 events and underperformed the seeding 6 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Lilian takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13, and finishing 33rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Lilian. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Lilian has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lilian is not just losing locally — Lilian is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 22% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Feng family travels cross-country so Lilian can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lilian has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lilian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13, which means Lilian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Lilian entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Lilian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 16 titles to the name, 74 entries, and 58 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lilian looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lilian's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lilian has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lilian. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

linda_zhao.name: Linda Zhao
linda_zhao.summary:
A Washington competitor, Linda Zhao stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U17 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Linda has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Linda is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 3 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Linda has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Charity Lam (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD
  • Annie Meng — 3 events in GD, best: #4

The 2023-2024 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 1 tournaments — a clear sign that Linda is still ascending.

Linda has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Linda has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Linda brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
linda_zhao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Linda Zhao, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Linda has 33 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 12% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Linda saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Linda burst onto the scene with a title in GD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Linda has delivered on some of those promises: 4 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Linda promised and what Linda has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Linda actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 3 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Linda plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 3 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Linda can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Linda has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Linda's texts. The go-to partner is Coco Cenke Tao (6 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15 — we hope Alex Anlai Song has emotionally recovered.

Of Linda's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Linda feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals, Linda has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Linda has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Linda is not just losing locally — Linda is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhao family travels cross-country so Linda can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Linda has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Linda showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Linda traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Linda entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Linda paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 33 entries, and 29 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Linda looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Linda's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Linda has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Linda. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

linden_wang.name: Linden Wang
linden_wang.summary:
A California competitor, Linden Wang has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (Men's Singles, U17 BD, U17 XD, U17 BS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 9 career titles across 20 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Linden is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Linden especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 3 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (Men's Singles, U17 BD, U17 XD, U17 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BS U15, XD U15) • 5 ORC titles.

Linden has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Eva Wang (current primary partner) — 7 events, 1 title together in XD, spanning 4 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Linden has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Linden has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Linden has been seeded #1 or #2 in 11 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 9 titles, Linden has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
linden_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Linden Wang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Linden is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Linden has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Linden would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Linden, we kept reading — all 51 entries across 20 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 18% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Linden held up a trophy, there were roughly 4 other times Linden walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Linden saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Linden burst onto the scene with a title in BS U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Linden has delivered on some of those promises: 9 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Linden promised and what Linden has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Linden actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Linden is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Linden experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Linden has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Linden entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Linden does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Linden has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 9 wins means Linden is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Linden plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Linden can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Linden has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Linden's texts. The go-to partner is Eva Wang (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Eva Wang has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Linden has been seeded in 15 events and underperformed the seeding 8 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Linden takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U19, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Linden. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Linden of is playing it safe. Linden has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Linden has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 6 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Linden has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Linden is not just losing locally — Linden is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 18% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Linden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 NorCal CRC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Linden has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Linden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U17, which means Linden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NorCal CRC, where Linden entered 2 events with results ranging from 1st to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Linden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 51 entries, and 42 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Linden looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Linden's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Linden has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Linden. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

lindsey_li.name: Lindsey Li
lindsey_li.summary:
Out of California, Lindsey Li is a Regional Championship titlist, capturing an impressive 3 ORC titles across 2 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Lindsey as one of the premier players on the circuit.

Notably, Lindsey has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Lindsey is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 3 ORC titles.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Lindsey adapts to anyone:
  • Jason Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (4 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Camellia Wang (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in GD/U13, across 2 seasons)
  • Selina Xu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #2)

The 2022-2023 season has been a step up, with 3 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Lindsey is still ascending.

Lindsey has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NW, NorCal, Lindsey isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Lindsey's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
lindsey_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lindsey Li, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 23 career entries in 8 tournaments, Lindsey has 4 titles — a 17% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 4 entries where Lindsey drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 23 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Lindsey saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lindsey's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2022-2023 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 12 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lindsey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Lindsey can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Lindsey has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lindsey's texts. The go-to partner is Jason Yang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Alex Wen has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Lindsey entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Lindsey's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Lindsey and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Lindsey has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Lindsey has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Lindsey has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lindsey has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lindsey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U13, which means Lindsey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-25 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Lindsey entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 25th. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Lindsey paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 23 entries, and 19 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lindsey looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lindsey's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lindsey has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lindsey. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

lindsey_wang.name: Lindsey Wang
lindsey_wang.summary:
Out of Illinois, Lindsey Wang is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U19 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Lindsey especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
lindsey_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lindsey Wang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Lindsey is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Lindsey has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Lindsey saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lindsey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Lindsey is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Lindsey wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Lindsey, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

linus_yong.name: Linus Yong
linus_yong.summary:
Hailing from California, Linus Yong is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U15 level, capturing 2 titles across 12 tournaments and 28 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Linus keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Linus is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Linus competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Linus has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Enming Cao (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

Linus has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Linus is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
linus_yong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Linus Yong, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 wins across 28 entries in 12 tournaments. That is a 7% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Linus shows up to a tournament, Linus pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Linus saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Linus's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 8 entries — a stretch where Linus actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Linus is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Linus experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Linus has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Linus does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Linus has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Linus is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Linus plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Linus can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Linus has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Linus's texts. The go-to partner is Yian Yang (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Yian Yang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Linus's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

One thing nobody can accuse Linus of is playing it safe. Linus has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Linus has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Linus has built a permanent residence on that line. Linus has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Linus has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Linus showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Linus traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Linus entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Linus paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 28 entries, and 26 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Linus looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Linus's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Linus has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Linus. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
