lisa_liu.name: Lisa Liu
lisa_liu.summary:
A Maryland competitor, Lisa Liu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 8 entries in 4 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Lisa has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Lisa competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Lisa has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Lisa brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
lisa_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lisa Liu, from Maryland, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Lisa is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 NE OLC (NVBC), and so far, the only thing Lisa has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Lisa saga began at 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lisa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Lisa has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Lisa has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lisa has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lisa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in GS U13, which means Lisa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Lisa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Lisa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Lisa keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Lisa has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Lisa, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Lisa. We will be here, taking notes.

liyu_zheng.name: Liyu Zheng
liyu_zheng.summary:
Out of Texas, Liyu Zheng has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 19 entries in 9 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Liyu is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Liyu has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Liyu brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
liyu_zheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Liyu Zheng, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Liyu has 19 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Liyu saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Liyu plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Liyu has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Liyu's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U15 — we hope Neel R Reddy has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Liyu has shown up 4 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Liyu has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Liyu has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Liyu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, which means Liyu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Liyu entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Liyu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 19 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Liyu. Liyu just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Liyu has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Liyu. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Liyu: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

logan_lu.name: Logan Lu
logan_lu.summary:
Representing California, Logan Lu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 32 entries in 12 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Logan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Logan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Alice Luo (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (6 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Sriyansh Chigullapally (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events)

Logan has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Logan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Logan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
logan_lu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Logan Lu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 32 career entries in 12 tournaments, Logan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 31 entries where Logan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 32 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Logan saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Logan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 32 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Logan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Logan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alice Luo: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alice Luo has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Laasya Gurram has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Logan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Logan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Logan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Logan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Logan is not just losing locally — Logan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lu family travels cross-country so Logan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Logan has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Logan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Logan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Logan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Logan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 32 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Logan. Logan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Logan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Logan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Logan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

lohith_reddy_arava.name: Lohith Reddy Arava
lohith_reddy_arava.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Lohith Reddy Arava broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 24 entries across 13 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Lohith has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Lohith competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Lohith has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Vishwajeeth Madhavan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5)

Having moved up to U15, Lohith is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Lohith brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Lohith has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Lohith brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
lohith_reddy_arava.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lohith Reddy Arava, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Lohith has 24 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Lohith saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Peak Sports South OLC in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lohith's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 8 entries — a stretch where Lohith actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Lohith is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

On the doubles front, Lohith has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Kartik Kanchi (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Gagan Polineni has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Lohith's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Lohith has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Lohith has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Lohith has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lohith has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lohith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Lohith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Lohith entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Lohith paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 24 entries, and 23 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lohith looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lohith's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lohith has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lohith. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The Lokaditya saga began at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Lokaditya is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Lokaditya's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Lokaditya is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Lokaditya is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Lokaditya wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Lokaditya, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Lokesh is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Rithika Thatipatri (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Vishanth Vijay (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)
  • Meghna Yeluguri (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Lokesh has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Lokesh has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Lokesh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
lokesh_ravuri.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lokesh Ravuri, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Lokesh has 61 career entries across 23 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Lokesh saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lokesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 61 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 29 of them, to be precise. Lokesh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lokesh's texts. The go-to partner is Dhruvan Karthik (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Varshini Subramanian has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Lokesh has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lokesh is not just losing locally — Lokesh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ravuri family travels cross-country so Lokesh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lokesh has 48 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lokesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Lokesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Lokesh entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 97th. That is an average placement of 59th, which is a polite way of saying Lokesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 61 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Lokesh. Lokesh just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Lokesh has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Lokesh. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Lokesh: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

lucas_liu.name: Lucas Liu
lucas_liu.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Lucas Liu embodies the relentless spirit of junior badminton, currently competing at the U15 level, with 44 career entries across 18 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Lucas's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

Notably, Lucas has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Lucas especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Lucas has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Shreya S Kirikera (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Jason Yiheng Luo (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Lucas has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Lucas has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Lucas brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
lucas_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lucas Liu, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Lucas has 44 career entries across 18 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Lucas saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lucas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 44 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Lucas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lucas's texts. The go-to partner is Shreya S Kirikera (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Venkata Sahasra Kondaveeti has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Lucas has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Lucas has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lucas is not just losing locally — Lucas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Lucas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lucas has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lucas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Lucas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Lucas entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Lucas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 44 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Lucas. Lucas just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Lucas has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Lucas. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Lucas: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

luka_wong.name: Luka Wong
luka_wong.summary:
Out of California, Luka Wong has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 13 entries in 7 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Luka competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Luka has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Luka has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Luka brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
luka_wong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Luka Wong, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 13 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 13-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Luka's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Luka keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Luka saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Luka has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Ryan Tiong has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Luka is not just losing locally — Luka is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wong family travels cross-country so Luka can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Luka has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Luka showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Luka traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Luka entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Luka paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Luka keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Luka has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Luka, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Luka. We will be here, taking notes.

lukas_gabriel_bayani.name: Lukas Gabriel Bayani
lukas_gabriel_bayani.summary:
Hailing from Maryland, Lukas Gabriel Bayani has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Lukas's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Lukas is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Lukas especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Lukas has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Medha Sivani Gudavalli (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Liam Xiao (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons
  • Zmy Gao (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Lukas has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Lukas isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Lukas is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
lukas_gabriel_bayani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lukas Gabriel Bayani, from Maryland, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 37 career entries in 14 tournaments, Lukas has 2 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 17 entries where Lukas drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 37 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Lukas saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 NE MassBad OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lukas's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lukas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Lukas is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Lukas and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Lukas needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Lukas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lukas's texts. The go-to partner is Medha Sivani Gudavalli (3 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Jida Wu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Lukas's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Lukas entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Lukas's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Lukas and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Lukas has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

One thing nobody can accuse Lukas of is playing it safe. Lukas has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Lukas has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Lukas has built a permanent residence on that line. Lukas has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lukas has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lukas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, which means Lukas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Lukas entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Lukas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 37 entries, and 35 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lukas looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lukas's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lukas has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lukas. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

lukas_rujia_chang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lukas Rujia Chang, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 43 career entries in 15 tournaments, Lukas has 2 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 20 entries where Lukas drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 43 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Lukas saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lukas's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 13 entries — a stretch where Lukas actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Lukas is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Lukas would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Lukas has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Lukas has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Lukas's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lukas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 2 titles came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Lukas is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Lukas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lukas's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Eric Cheng: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Eric Cheng has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19 — we hope Eric Cheng has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Lukas entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Lukas's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Lukas and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Lukas 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Lukas has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lukas is not just losing locally — Lukas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chang family travels cross-country so Lukas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lukas has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lukas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19, which means Lukas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Lukas entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Lukas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 43 entries, and 41 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lukas looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lukas's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lukas has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lukas. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

lukas_ye.name: Lukas Ye
lukas_ye.summary:
A Minnesota competitor, Lukas Ye has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 39 entries across 16 tournaments, Lukas is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Lukas competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Lukas adapts to anyone:
  • Victoria A Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (10 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Nathan Huang — a trusted BD partner (4 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

Lukas has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, SoCal, Lukas isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Lukas brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
lukas_ye.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lukas Ye, from Minnesota, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Lukas has 39 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Lukas saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lukas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 39 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Lukas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lukas's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Victoria A Liu: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Victoria A Liu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Victoria A Liu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Lukas has shown up 6 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Lukas has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lukas has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lukas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Lukas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Lukas entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 51st, which is a polite way of saying Lukas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 39 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Lukas. Lukas just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Lukas has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Lukas. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Lukas: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

luke_ai.name: Luke Ai
luke_ai.summary:
A California competitor, Luke Ai is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 12 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Luke is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Luke adapts to anyone:
  • Finley L Lin-Gracey (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Luke has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Luke brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
luke_ai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Luke Ai, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Luke's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Luke keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Luke saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Luke plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Luke has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Finley L Lin-Gracey has emotionally recovered.

Luke has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Luke has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Luke showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Luke traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Luke entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Luke paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Luke keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Luke has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Luke, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Luke. We will be here, taking notes.

luke_han.name: Luke Han
luke_han.summary:
Out of Illinois, Luke Han is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Luke in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Luke has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Luke is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 BD) • Junior Nationals top-3 (BS U11) • 2 ORC titles.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Luke adapts to anyone:
  • Jonathan Wu (current primary partner) — 18 events, 1 title together in BD/U13/U15, spanning 5 seasons
  • Felicia Ho — a trusted U15/XD partner (4 events, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Luke has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Luke has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Luke has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 4 titles, Luke has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
