luke_wen.name: Luke Wen
luke_wen.summary:
Out of Indiana, Luke Wen has established a commanding presence at the local tournament level, currently competing at the U15 level, racking up 5 titles across 10 tournaments. That winning pedigree at the local and club level creates the perfect launchpad for success at the regional and national stages.

Notably, Luke has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Luke especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Luke has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Pearl Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Adrian Zhang (current partner) — 2 events in BD, producing 2 titles together
  • Sahir Bhuiyan (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)
  • Ivy Chang (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 5 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Luke is still ascending.

Luke has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Luke isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Luke's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
luke_wen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Luke Wen, from Indiana, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 5 career titles, which sounds impressive until you learn that 5 of them came at local events where the draw is thinner than gas station coffee and the toughest opponent is someone's cousin who picked up a racket six months ago. A 18% win rate across 28 entries — big fish, very small pond, possibly a puddle. But Luke keeps entering, keeps competing, and keeps providing us with material. For that, we are grateful.

The Luke saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in BD U13 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Luke's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 5 titles in 16 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Luke experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Luke has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Peak Sports South OLC (Dec), where Luke entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Luke does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Luke has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 5 wins means Luke is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Luke plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Luke can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Luke has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Luke's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Pearl Li: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Pearl Li has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Lucas Lu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Luke's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Luke has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Luke has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Luke has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Luke showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Luke traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Luke entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Luke paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 28 entries, and 23 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Luke looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Luke's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Luke has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Luke. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

luna_han.name: Luna Han
luna_han.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Luna Han has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Luna's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Luna has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Luna especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Luna adapts to anyone:
  • Micah Wei (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Zoey Tian — 2 events in GD, best: #2, across 2 seasons
  • Aditi Gummadi — 7 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Luna has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Luna has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 6 seasons and 4 titles, Luna has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
luna_han.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Luna Han, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 49 career entries in 19 tournaments, Luna has 4 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Luna drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 49 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Luna saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Luna's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Luna actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Luna is 0-for-1. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Luna experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Luna has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Midwest OLC, where Luna entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Luna does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Luna has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Luna is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Luna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Luna can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Luna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Luna's texts. The go-to partner is Aditi Gummadi (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Micah Wei has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Luna's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Luna entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Luna's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Luna and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Luna has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Luna has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Luna is not just losing locally — Luna is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Han family travels cross-country so Luna can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Luna has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Luna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17, which means Luna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Luna entered 2 events with results ranging from 65th to 65th. That is an average placement of 65th, which is a polite way of saying Luna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 49 entries, and 45 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Luna looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Luna's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Luna has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Luna. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

luna_zhang.name: Luna Zhang
luna_zhang.summary:
Out of California, Luna Zhang has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 7 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Luna competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

In doubles, Luna has formed effective partnerships:
  • Andrew Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Qinxian Zhu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events)

Luna has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
luna_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Luna Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Luna is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 SoCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Luna has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Luna saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Luna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Luna has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Luna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U15, which means Luna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Luna entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Luna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Luna is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Luna wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Luna, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

lydia_chao.name: Lydia Chao
lydia_chao.summary:
Representing Maryland, Lydia Chao has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 XD, U17 GS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 9 career titles across 29 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Lydia is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (8 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Lydia is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 XD, U17 GS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (GD U19) • 3 ORC titles.

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Lydia adapts to anyone:
  • Charity Lam (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (10 total events in GD/U19, spanning 3 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 3 tournaments. Lydia is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Lydia has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Lydia has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 11 seeded entries across the career, Lydia consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 9 titles, Lydia has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
lydia_chao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Lydia Chao, from Maryland, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Lydia is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Lydia has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Lydia would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Lydia, we kept reading — all 82 entries across 29 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 11% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Lydia held up a trophy, there were roughly 8 other times Lydia walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Lydia saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Lydia's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Lydia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 8 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Lydia experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Lydia has reached 12 championship matches across this career. That is 12 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 12 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (8 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Lydia does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Lydia has never won anything. But 12 finals losses versus 9 wins means Lydia is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lydia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Lydia can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Lydia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lydia's texts. The go-to partner is Charity Lam (10 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Eshan Neog has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Lydia has been seeded in 11 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Lydia takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U17, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Lydia. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Lydia of is playing it safe. Lydia has entered older age groups at 8 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Lydia has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 9 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Lydia has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lydia is not just losing locally — Lydia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chao family travels cross-country so Lydia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lydia has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lydia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U15, which means Lydia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Lydia entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Lydia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 82 entries, and 73 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Lydia looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Lydia's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Lydia has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Lydia. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Lydia's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Lydia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 62 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Lydia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Lydia's texts. The go-to partner is Sophie Zhang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15 — we hope Kameran Hossain has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Lydia entered 4 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Lydia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Lydia and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Lydia has shown up 7 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Lydia has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Lydia is not just losing locally — Lydia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ng family travels cross-country so Lydia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Lydia has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Lydia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Lydia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Lydia entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Lydia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 62 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Lydia. Lydia just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Lydia has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Lydia. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Lydia: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
